title: Interesting Times Aren't a Curse — They're the New Operating Environment

source.original: 'Nothing Ever Happens' Is Over: The Fully Interconnected Startup — Naval Podcast

body_html:
Naval reframes the old saying may you live in interesting times not as a warning but as a situational briefing. The instinct to treat volatility as a temporary disruption — something to weather before returning to normal — is increasingly maladaptive. The interesting times are the new normal, and the sooner decision-makers internalize this, the better positioned they'll be.

What makes this era distinctly interesting is the asymmetry between creation and destruction. A small team can build a billion-dollar product. A small team can also, increasingly, access biological or kinetic capabilities that were once the exclusive province of state militaries. The leverage available to individuals and small groups has never been higher — in both directions.

This creates a paradox for optimists and pessimists alike. The optimist's case — that exponential technologies will solve humanity's biggest problems — is more plausible than ever. So is the pessimist's case — that those same technologies create existential risks. Both are simultaneously true, which is precisely what makes the moment so charged.

The practical response isn't paralysis or blind optimism. It's active navigation: building aggressively while maintaining situational awareness about how the same forces enabling your success could enable someone else's destructive act. It's holding both possibilities in mind without collapsing into either.

Anyone still operating on the assumption that the world will return to a stable, predictable baseline is making a dangerous bet. The evidence — from geopolitics to technology to biology — points in one direction: increasing complexity, increasing speed, and increasing stakes. The only rational posture is engagement with eyes open.
